mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M-Class CDI engine typically costs £2,000–£7,500 supply only depending on generation. OM612 5-cylinder engines (ML270) sit at the lower end (£2,000–£5,000), while OM642 V6 units (ML320) run £2,500–£7,500. A replacement engine can cost between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engines typically at the higher end of the range. Dealers charge £4,000–£7,000+. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine generation your M-Class has. The OM612 (W163 ML270, 1998–2005) is a robust 5-cylinder but has well-documented issues: injector seal failure (the dreaded 'black death'), low-pressure fuel pump failure, and oil leaks. The OM642 (W164 ML320, 2005–2011) is a smooth V6 but has oil cooler seal leaks, turbo actuator failure, EGR clogging and swirl flap issues. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM612 injector seal failure ('black death' — carbon buildup, chuffing, diesel smell), OM612 low-pressure fuel pump failure (particles damaging high-pressure pump), OM642 oil cooler seal leaks (oil loss, puddles, 'Oil Level Low' warnings), OM642 turbo actuator failure (loss of power, no boost), OM642 swirl flap failure (loss of power), and EGR clogging on both engines. The OM642's oil cooler is deep in the V of the engine, making seal replacement a big job.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your M-Class is worth £4,000–£10,000 and a recon engine is £3,500–£5,000 (35–100% of the car's value), assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork and interior justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust may not. W163 and early W164 models are becoming classics — a good engine rebuild can add value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W163 (1998–2005) ML270 CDI is OM612.963 (2.7L I5, 163 PS); W164 (2005–2007) ML320 CDI is OM642.920 (3.0L V6, 224 PS); W164 (2007–2011) ML320 CDI is OM642.820 (3.0L V6, 224 PS). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM612 injector seals ('black death'), or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Injector seal replacement (£300–£800) is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement. The copper seals are 'pennies from Mercedes'. If caught early, the job is straightforward — replace the copper seal and recut the seat in the head. If ignored, injectors can become 'properly welded in'. At the first sign of chuffing, ticking or diesel smell, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates. A replacement engine can cost between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engines typically at the higher end of the range. Used OM612 engines are available from around £1,500–£2,500. Reconditioned OM642 engines command a premium due to the complexity of the V6 design. Dealer quotes can range from £4,500 to £7,000+.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an M-Class CDI engine typically takes 10–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The OM642 V6 requires more labour than the inline-five OM612. The oil cooler sits deep in the V of the OM642 engine and is a big job if the seals need replacing.

cta.label: → Compare ML270 / ML320 CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: OM612 Injector Seal Failure — "Black Death" (ML270 CDI, W163 1998–2005)
problems.symptoms: Carbon buildup ("black death") around injectors, chuffing or ticking sound from injector area, diesel smell in cabin, rough idle, hard starting, black smoke. The copper seal underneath each injector fails, allowing combustion gases to escape and causing carbon deposits to form. The "20 cent washer on the injector fails causing black gunk to form".
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — injectors "get tired by about 150k miles".
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (injector seal replacement, seat recutting). The copper seals are "pennies from Mercedes".
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, injectors can seize in the head, requiring removal with a slide hammer.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear chuffing or smell diesel, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The OM612's injector seals are its most common and well-documented failure. The dreaded "black death" occurs when the copper seal fails — carbon and diesel build up under the rocker cover. If caught early, the job is straightforward — replace the copper seal and recut the seat in the head. If ignored, injectors can become "properly welded in". At the first sign of chuffing, ticking or diesel smell, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (ML320 CDI, W164 2005–2011)
problems.symptoms: "Check Oil Level" or "Oil Level Low" message, decreasing oil level, oil stains or puddles when parked, oil at the bottom of the transmission bell housing. The oil cooler sits deep in the V of the OM642 engine. The leak can be misdiagnosed as a rear main seal leak.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — a design flaw with extreme temperature and vibration. Later models have improved sealing material, but the issue is not fully removed.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (oil cooler seals, full seal kit, labour). A £5 part and £800–£900 labour to replace due to its location.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil loss can lead to engine damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see oil warnings or puddles, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The OM642's oil cooler seal leak is one of the most common and serious issues on this engine. The oil cooler is located deep in the V of the engine — accessing it requires significant labour. Mercedes used gaskets of an inferior material, which after some miles and years will result in an oily mess. If you notice any oil loss or see oil stains, have the seals inspected immediately. Use the updated sealing material for replacement.

problems.title: OM612 Low-Pressure Fuel Pump Failure (ML270 CDI, W163 1998–2005)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, hard starting, fuel pump whine, engine stalling. The low-pressure fuel pump can fail, with particles damaging the high-pressure pump. Air bubbles in fuel lines due to hardened O-rings is also a common issue.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, particles can damage the high-pressure pump and injectors.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The OM612's low-pressure fuel pump can fail, sending particles through the system that can damage the high-pressure pump. If you notice fuel pump whine, hard starting or loss of power, have the system inspected promptly. Replacing the low-pressure pump and O-rings is significantly cheaper than a full high-pressure pump and injector replacement.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: ML270 / ML320 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch early — copper seals are cheap, labour is moderate
Essential repair — prevent damage to high-pressure pump
Essential repair — catch before oil loss damages engine
Actuator refurbishment is cost-effective; full turbo if damaged
Swirl flaps can stick or break — repair to restore power
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This ML270 / ML320 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * W163 ML270 (OM612) with full service history and no injector "black death" — robust 5-cylinder
* OM612 with injector seals already replaced and no fuel pump issues
* W164 ML320 (OM642) with oil cooler seals replaced with updated material
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
avoidIf: * OM612 with chuffing or ticking from injectors — "black death" injector seal failure
* OM612 with fuel pump whine or hard starting — low-pressure fuel pump failure
* OM642 with oil leaks or "Oil Level Low" warnings — oil cooler seals are failing
* OM642 with loss of power or boost — turbo actuator or swirl flap issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M-Class CDI range spans two distinct engine families. The OM612 (ML270, W163 1998–2005) is a robust 5-cylinder but has well-documented injector seal and fuel pump issues. The OM642 (ML320, W164 2005–2011) is a smooth V6 but has oil cooler seal, turbo actuator and swirl flap vulnerabilities. With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £3,500–£5,000 reconditioned engine on an M-Class valued at £4,000–£10,000 is 35–100% of the car's value — assess the overall condition carefully. W163 and early W164 models are becoming classics; a good engine rebuild can add value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

cta.label: → Compare ML270 / ML320 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz ML270 / ML320 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Injector Seals — On OM612, check for carbon buildup around injectors ("black death") and listen for chuffing or ticking
* Fuel Pump — On OM612, listen for fuel pump whine; check for hard starting
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (timing chain wear on both engines)
* Oil Level — On OM642, check for "Oil Level Low" warnings or oil puddles (oil cooler seal leak)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM612/OM642) and oil-interval discipline
* Turbo Check — On OM642, test acceleration for power loss or boost issues (turbo actuator)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
